Commendation

How Signal works

The game

Signal puts you inside your own nervous system. You pilot through real situations — school, social pressure, recovery — and make choices that have actual mechanical consequences. Your Health and Capacity gauges change with every decision and carry forward through the whole flight.

Health vs Capacity

Health (HLT) — your physical and psychological wellbeing. Depletes under sustained demand, restores with genuine recovery.

Capacity (CAP) — your nervous system's available processing resources right now. More volatile. Drops fast under load stacking, restores with low-input windows. If Capacity hits the floor — burnout.

Your signal profile

The five profile questions map your nervous system's cost structure across Sensory Input, Social-Relational, Processing-Integration, Emotional Intensity, and Transition-Change. Your profile changes the story text at every step — high-signal players get different situations, different costs, different feedback than lower-signal players.

Flights

Each flight covers a different day-type: School Day (full cycle from reboot to close-out), Social Demand Day (masking cost, interpersonal load, recovery), Recovery Day (post-overload, guilt trap, pacing). Each is ~25–35 minutes of real play time.

Wings

Commendations earned by landing with specific outcomes — clean descent, protecting the reboot window, completing high-difficulty flights without burnout. Saved to your logbook.

No wrong answers

Every choice has real tradeoffs. Some protect Capacity at a relational cost. Some protect the relationship at a Capacity cost. The point isn't to win — it's to understand what each choice actually costs and why.

Signal
a nervous system game

Your nervous system isn't broken.
It's calibrated differently — and that has a logic.

~30 min per flight · no login · no wrong answers

Before we fly

Here's what's actually happening

This game is built on real nervous system science. Not metaphors. The actual mechanism.

Load

Your nervous system processes every input, demand, and experience around you — constantly, simultaneously, whether you want it to or not. That processing costs resources. Those resources are real and finite. The word for what you spend is load.

Capacity

Your nervous system has a limit to how much load it can handle at once. That limit is capacity. It shifts constantly — based on sleep, baseline stress, what's already happened today. When load exceeds capacity, the system starts failing in a specific, predictable sequence.

The failure sequence

Cognition goes first: thinking gets foggy, processing slows. Then emotional regulation: small things hit harder, reactions are bigger than the situation warrants. Then communication: finding words gets hard, conversations feel impossible. Then shutdown or meltdown — full system failure. This is not a character flaw. It is a biological sequence.

Mismatch

Not all nervous systems have the same capacity in every area. When the environment is built for a different type of system, the gap between what's demanded and what your system can supply is called mismatch. Sustained mismatch produces the failure sequence above. The problem is not your brain. The problem is the gap.

The framework

Load has domains. They stack.

Your nervous system isn't handling one thing at a time. It's running five types of load simultaneously — and when they compound, the cost isn't additive. It multiplies.

Sensory Input Load
Every sound, light, texture, smell, temperature change, and physical sensation your system is tracking — whether you're conscious of it or not. High-signal systems pick up more, filter less, and spend more resources just existing in demanding environments.
High looks like: fluorescent lights feeling physically wrong, needing quiet after crowds, exhaustion in busy spaces that others don't seem to feel.
Social-Relational Load
Every social interaction requires real nervous system resources: reading tone, tracking subtext, managing your presentation, monitoring others. For some systems, the cost per interaction is higher. Recovery isn't optional — it's physiologically necessary.
High looks like: alone time feeling non-negotiable, being drained after groups even when you enjoyed them, nothing left after a day of people.
Processing-Integration Load
After receiving information, your system has to interpret, organize, connect to existing knowledge, and form a response. Some systems do this rapidly. Others deeply and slowly. Neither is better — they cost differently.
High looks like: knowing the answer but not getting it out fast, brain buffering under pressure, needing to sit with things before responding.
Emotional-Intensity Load
Emotional experience uses nervous system bandwidth to process, regulate, and integrate. High-intensity systems experience more deeply, pick up others' emotional states accurately, and carry emotional residue longer. This is bandwidth allocation, not instability.
High looks like: feedback landing heavier than it should, emotions disproportionate to the trigger, difficulty shaking feelings once they arrive.
Transition-Change Load
Every change in context, plan, or environment requires your system to recalibrate its current model of the situation. For some systems, this recalibration is more expensive and takes longer. Surprises hit harder and need more recovery time.
High looks like: even minor changes producing real stress spikes, needing a beat after task-switching, preferring to know what's coming.
Why this matters

These domains stack. That's the thing.

Load Stacking

When multiple domains are active simultaneously, the cost isn't additive — it compounds. Sensory load at a 6 plus social load at a 5 doesn't equal load-11. It can feel like load-20, because your system is trying to run all those processes at the same time on the same limited capacity.

Example: School lunch period

Noise + Lights
+
Social Demands
+
Incoming Emotions
=
Near Limit

Baseline Drain

In a high-mismatch environment like school, your capacity is already partially depleted before any acute moment happens. The thing that finally tips you over isn't always the actual problem. It's just the last thing.

The part school doesn't tell you: You often can't change these environments. You can't make the cafeteria quieter or leave the group project. That doesn't mean you're stuck with the damage. Understanding your load lets you pace, plan recovery, and stop blaming yourself when a stacked environment crashes your system.
Your profile · 1 of 5
Sensory Input

You walk into a packed, loud cafeteria. What happens to your system?

This maps your sensory input domain — how much your nervous system tracks and processes environmental stimuli, and what it costs when that volume is high.

Your profile · 2 of 5
Social-Relational

After a full day of being around people — what does your nervous system actually need?

Social interaction uses real nervous system resources. This question maps the cost-per-hour of social engagement for your system — not your personality, the actual resource expenditure.

Your profile · 3 of 5
Processing-Integration

A teacher calls on you. You know the answer. What happens between knowing and saying?

This maps the integration step — the gap between receiving information and producing output. Some systems run this pathway fast and serially. Others run it slowly and deeply. Neither is better. They cost differently and perform differently under pressure.

Your profile · 4 of 5
Emotional-Intensity

Someone gives you minor feedback on your work. What does your nervous system do with it?

Emotional intensity is not about being dramatic. It's about how much processing bandwidth your system allocates to emotional information — your own and others'. High-intensity systems pick up more, process more, and carry more. That's not instability. It's a different bandwidth allocation.

Your profile · 5 of 5
Transition-Change

Plans just changed. Something you'd mentally prepared for is now different. What does your system do?

Transitions require your nervous system to recalibrate its current model of the situation. This costs different amounts for different systems — and the cost is proportionate to how much the system had already allocated to the original expectation, not to how significant the change appears from the outside.

Your profile

Your signal

This is your nervous system's cost structure across the five load domains. Higher bars mean your system runs more actively in that area — more processing, more spend, more recovery required. Not worse. Different.

What this isn't: a diagnosis, a fixed identity, or a ranking. It's a snapshot of your nervous system's current cost structure. Two people can have completely different profiles and both be entirely normal. The variation is the point.

Zone note

For your specific system

Carry-forward:
No wrong answer. Every option carried real tradeoffs. The point is understanding what each one actually cost.

System Overload

Your capacity hit the floor. The nervous system entered protection mode — the same sequence that produces shutdown, meltdown, or complete inability to function in normal ways.

What this represents

When capacity reaches zero, the nervous system's only remaining move is to stop processing non-essential demands. This isn't weakness or failure. It's the system protecting itself after running past its limit for too long without adequate recovery. The question isn't why it happened — it's what the conditions were that made it inevitable.

What recovery actually requires

Post-burnout recovery is not rest in the conventional sense. It requires active reduction of all domain loads simultaneously: low-sensory environment, no social demand, minimal processing requirements, and time — often more time than feels acceptable. Pushing back in before the system is ready extends the recovery period significantly.

⚡ What your system needs

The thing school never says

You are navigating environments that were not designed for your nervous system. That produces real, predictable costs. None of this is a character flaw. It is a mismatch between your system's calibration and the environments you're required to be in. Understanding the mismatch doesn't fix the school. It does mean you stop blaming yourself for the bill.

Session Tools

Generate a session link for a client, or decrypt a full clinical result they send back. No data is stored anywhere — everything lives in the URL.
